professor.name: Dr. Okafor
professor.text: We've been discussing how cities use their limited downtown land this semester. Several mid-sized cities have begun tearing out ground-level parking lots and replacing them with public plazas and pocket parks. Some urban planners argue this revives street life and draws foot traffic to local shops, while others worry it pushes away visitors who depend on cars and hurts the businesses that need easy parking. Should cities convert downtown parking lots into public plazas? Why or why not?

students.name: Claire
students.text: I think converting these lots is worth it. A plaza concentrates pedestrians in one place, and that visibility tends to benefit the small cafés and shops nearby far more than a row of parked cars ever could. The liveliness itself becomes a reason for people to keep coming back.

students.name: Paul
students.text: I'm not convinced it works everywhere. In cities without reliable public transit, removing parking simply discourages anyone living outside walking distance from coming downtown at all. The businesses most likely to suffer are exactly the ones that depend on customers driving in from the suburbs.

professor.name: Dr. Lin
professor.text: We've been studying how cities manage crowding on public transit this semester. A few transit agencies have started charging higher fares during rush hour and offering discounts during off-peak times, hoping to spread demand more evenly across the day. Some economists argue this eases dangerous overcrowding and rewards flexible riders, while others contend it unfairly burdens shift workers who have no choice about when they travel. Do you think transit systems should adopt peak-hour pricing?

students.name: Mia
students.text: I support peak pricing because it puts a real signal on a genuinely scarce resource. When trains are dangerously packed at eight in the morning, a modest surcharge nudges the riders who can shift their schedule, and that relief makes the commute safer for everyone who cannot.

students.name: Carlos
students.text: The problem is that flexibility is itself a luxury. Hospital staff, factory workers, and early-shift cleaners cannot simply choose a cheaper hour, so the policy quietly taxes the people who can least afford it while sparing office workers with adjustable, forgiving schedules.

professor.name: Dr. Hassan
professor.text: More and more schools are experimenting with dropping letter grades entirely and replacing them with detailed written feedback on each assignment. Supporters say these narratives capture a student's real progress and reduce the anxiety that comes with constant competition, while critics insist that without grades students lose a clear benchmark and universities struggle to compare applicants fairly. Should schools replace letter grades with written narrative feedback? Why or why not?

students.name: Yuki
students.text: Narrative feedback is far more useful for actual learning. A single letter compresses an entire semester into one symbol, telling a student almost nothing about what to fix, whereas a paragraph can pinpoint the exact reasoning that went wrong and explain precisely how to improve it.

students.name: Ross
students.text: I think grades still serve a purpose we shouldn't dismiss too quickly. They give a quick, comparable standard that motivates many students and helps overloaded admissions offices evaluate thousands of applicants. Replacing them with long narratives sounds ideal but becomes deeply impractical at scale.

professor.name: Dr. Novak
professor.text: More and more social platforms now build their entire experience around algorithmic feeds that decide what each user sees. Regulators in several countries are pushing companies to offer a simple switch back to a plain chronological timeline. Some argue users deserve that control and transparency over their own attention, while others say most people clearly prefer the curated feed and rarely touch such settings. Should platforms be required to offer a non-algorithmic feed option?

students.name: Lena
students.text: Requiring the option is fundamentally about user autonomy. Even if only a minority ever flips the switch, people have a right to step outside a system engineered to maximize their attention, and that transparency pressures companies to design feeds that genuinely serve users.

students.name: Cameron
students.text: I doubt a mandate accomplishes much in practice. The vast majority of people leave default settings untouched, so the requirement mainly creates compliance work while changing little. Companies could also design the chronological option to feel deliberately worse, quietly undermining the whole point.
